rejected_readings.label: passive_word_labels
rejected_readings.rendering: Sciences, crafts and arts have their own structure independent of word; the word merely labels or describes them.
rejected_readings.why: Demotes vāk from the absolute binding principle to a passive property or linguistic labeling device, contradicting the commentary's insistence that word-power actively binds (upabandhati) all domains of human competence. The sort error (absolute vs property) makes the binding relation impossible.

rejected_readings.label: self_differentiated_artifacts
rejected_readings.rendering: Artifacts differentiate themselves into parts through their own nature; word plays no role.
rejected_readings.why: Severs the word's causal role in differentiation (vibhajanam). The commentary explicitly states that articulation of the craft-object is word-borne, and the artifact's very identity as this kind of thing depends on the word-guided maker's projection of rationale onto matter. The claimed relation (vibhajanam depends on vastu's own nature) is not derivable from axioms establishing that vibhajanam is instead word-borne.

rejected_readings.label: word_secondary_effect
rejected_readings.rendering: Things are first brought to completion, then the word simply reflects or records their articulation.
rejected_readings.why: Reverses the temporal and causal order. The commentary makes clear that differentiation (vibhajanam) happens under word's control (tad-vasat), not after. The word-guided maker projects the rationale onto matter; differentiation is not a secondary labeling.

notes: The verse generalizes vāk's function from the previous unit (1.118–1.121 on word's role in manufacture) to encompass all domains of human culture: theory, technique, and aesthetics. Entities are bound together by word; their products are differentiated under word's control. The contract emphasizes vāk as absolute principle (not merely a linguistic tool) and captures the bidirectional dependency: sciences/crafts/arts are bound by word, and their artifacts' articulation is word-borne.
